skill.name: on-call-handoff-patterns
skill.description: Create reliable on-call handoffs with shift summaries, incident context, escalation paths, and readiness checklists.
skill.summary: Create complete on-call handoffs for shift changes, active incidents, and support rotations.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, weak cryptography, scheduling, and reconnaissance patterns in SKILL.md. AI review found these are documentation examples, Markdown fences, timestamps, operational checklist text, and reference links, with no executable code, prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Fence Markers Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: The external command findings point to Markdown code fence boundaries and documentation examples. They do not invoke Ruby backticks or execute shell commands from the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are Markdown fences or inline example text in a documentation skill. No executable Ruby or shell wrapper is present in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reference Links Flagged as Network Activity
low_findings.description: The network findings are static links to dashboards, internal documents, Slack, PagerDuty, and public learning resources. The skill contains no fetch, curl, webhook, or automatic network request logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Every network location is a Markdown link in template or resources sections. I found no code path that transmits data.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern Found in Non-Cryptographic Text
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings occur in the skill description and ordinary infrastructure prose. These lines do not define or call cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptive operational text, not hash functions or encryption APIs. No weak cryptographic behavior is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Scheduling Command Pattern Matched Timestamp Text
low_findings.description: The at command scheduling finding points to a status update time inside an incident handoff template. It does not schedule a command or instruct automation to run later.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is an incident communication timestamp in Markdown. There is no command syntax or shell execution context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Pattern Matched Checklist Language
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance findings point to access verification and documentation checklist wording. The skill does not run discovery commands or scan networks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are prose in checklist sections. No reconnaissance tool, network scan command, or target enumeration logic is present.

content.value_statement: On-call transitions often lose incident context, ownership, and escalation details. This skill provides reusable handoff templates and checklists for clearer support continuity.
actual_capabilities: Provides full, quick, and mid-incident handoff templates.
Defines handoff sections for active incidents, investigations, changes, known issues, and upcoming events.
Includes incoming and outgoing engineer checklist items.
Provides a short agenda for synchronous handoff meetings.
Lists escalation triggers and escalation handoff expectations.
Suggests pre-shift, during-shift, and post-shift operating routines.
limitations: Does not connect to PagerDuty, Slack, Grafana, or ticketing systems.
Does not validate real incident data or service ownership.
Requires teams to adapt examples to their tools and escalation policies.
Does not replace formal incident command or postmortem processes.

use_cases.title: Prepare a weekly platform on-call handoff
use_cases.description: Create a complete shift summary with active issues, recent changes, watch items, and next steps.

use_cases.title: Transfer an active incident during a shift change
use_cases.description: Summarize current impact, actions taken, owners, communications, and required next decisions.

use_cases.title: Standardize team handoff quality
use_cases.description: Build repeatable checklist and meeting formats for engineers joining an on-call rotation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic shift handoff
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner handoff for a normal shift change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an on-call handoff for my shift. Include active incidents, ongoing investigations, recent changes, known issues, upcoming events, and incoming engineer checks.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a noisy shift
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate handoff after alerts and investigations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn these notes into a clear on-call handoff. Separate resolved alerts, unresolved investigations, risk areas, owners, next steps, and escalation contacts.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a mid-incident transfer
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced handoff while an incident is still active.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a mid-incident handoff for this active issue. Include current state, customer impact, actions taken, hypotheses, communication status, owners, and immediate next decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Design a team handoff process
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced process design for an on-call rotation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a standardized on-call handoff process for my team. Include document structure, overlap timing, meeting agenda, required fields, quality checks, and common failure modes.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer needs to hand off two investigations and one upcoming database maintenance window.
output_examples.output: A structured shift summary with watch items, owners, dashboards, maintenance timing, escalation contacts, and incoming engineer checklist items.

output_examples.input: An incident commander must transfer a payment degradation incident during mitigation.
output_examples.output: A focused incident handoff covering current severity, customer impact, mitigation status, communication updates, key people, and next actions.

output_examples.input: A manager wants a consistent handoff process for a new rotation.
output_examples.output: A reusable process outline with overlap timing, meeting agenda, required handoff sections, readiness checks, and escalation expectations.

best_practices: Keep handoffs specific, current, and tied to owners or next actions.
Use a synchronous overlap for active incidents or unclear investigations.
Verify alert routing, access, and escalation contacts before the incoming shift starts.
anti_patterns: Do not hand off active issues without impact, status, and next-step details.
Do not rely only on chat history for incident context transfer.
Do not omit known risks, upcoming maintenance, or recent deployments.

faq.question: Does this skill execute commands or access production systems?
faq.answer: No. It provides documentation templates and process guidance only.

faq.question: Can I use it for active incidents?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a mid-incident handoff format for transferring context during mitigation.

faq.question: Does it integrate with PagerDuty or Slack?
faq.answer: No. It references common tools as examples but does not connect to external services.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: It is useful for SREs, DevOps engineers, incident commanders, and managers who run on-call rotations.

faq.question: Can the templates be customized?
faq.answer: Yes. Teams should adjust fields, escalation paths, and examples to match their operating model.

faq.question: What should I provide before using it?
faq.answer: Provide current incidents, investigations, recent changes, upcoming events, owners, and escalation contacts.
